words.definition: a sign that indicates you have a particular illness
example_sentences: The main symptoms of flu are high fever and extreme fatigue.
She went to the doctor when her symptoms got significantly worse.
These are classic symptoms of severe dehydration.
words.pronunciation_tip: SIMP-tum — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'sign of illness' every time — say 'symptom'.

words.definition: a written instruction from a doctor for medication
example_sentences: The doctor gave me a prescription for a course of antibiotics.
You need a prescription to obtain this particular medicine.
She collected her monthly prescription from the pharmacy.
words.pronunciation_tip: prih-SKRIP-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a letter from the doctor for medicine' — say 'a prescription'.

words.definition: to identify what illness or condition someone has
example_sentences: She was diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes last year.
The doctor diagnosed a severe vitamin D deficiency.
It took several months to correctly diagnose the condition.
words.pronunciation_tip: dy-ug-NOHZ — three syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the doctor found my illness' — say 'the doctor diagnosed me with...'.

words.definition: the process of getting better after illness or injury
example_sentences: She made a remarkably full recovery after the major operation.
Recovery from spinal surgery can take six months or longer.
His recovery was much faster than the doctors had expected.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-KUV-er-ee — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he became healthy again' as a noun — say 'his recovery was rapid'.

words.definition: a condition that lasts a very long time or keeps returning
example_sentences: She has suffered from chronic back pain for over a decade.
Chronic stress is genuinely harmful to your long-term health.
It's a chronic condition that can be managed but never fully cured.
words.pronunciation_tip: KRON-ik — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'long-term illness' every time — say 'chronic condition'.

words.definition: the body's natural defence system against disease and infection
example_sentences: Regular exercise significantly strengthens your immune system.
His immune system was seriously weakened by months of stress.
A good balanced diet is essential for a healthy immune system.
words.pronunciation_tip: ih-MYOON SIS-tum.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'my body's defence' — say 'my immune system'.
words.collocations: ['boost immune system', 'strong immune system', 'weaken immune system']

words.definition: the physical or mental ability to do something demanding for a long time
example_sentences: Running long distances requires remarkable physical stamina.
She has incredible stamina — she can work productively all day.
Regular training will gradually build up your stamina.
words.pronunciation_tip: STAM-ih-nuh — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'energy to continue' — say 'stamina'.

words.definition: an abnormal medical reaction to a particular substance
example_sentences: She has a severe and potentially life-threatening allergy to peanuts.
Hay fever is an extremely common seasonal allergy.
His dust allergy causes him to sneeze constantly in old buildings.
words.pronunciation_tip: AL-er-jee — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have a problem with nuts' — say 'I have a nut allergy'.

words.definition: extreme tiredness, often as a result of physical or mental effort
example_sentences: She was suffering from complete exhaustion and severe fatigue.
Fatigue is a very common and debilitating symptom of anaemia.
The long international journey caused significant fatigue.
words.pronunciation_tip: fuh-TEEG — two syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am very tired' always — 'fatigue' is more extreme and often medical.

words.definition: to seek advice or information from a professional
example_sentences: You should always consult a doctor before changing your diet.
She consulted a specialist about her persistent back pain.
Always consult a qualified professional before taking new medication.
words.pronunciation_tip: kun-SULT — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I asked the doctor' formally — say 'I consulted a doctor'.

words.definition: easily spread from one person to another
example_sentences: Chickenpox is highly contagious and spreads very easily.
Please stay at home if you have a contagious illness.
His infectious enthusiasm is almost as contagious as a cold.
words.pronunciation_tip: kun-TAY-jus — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse contagious (spread by contact) with infectious (spread through air).

words.definition: the specific amount of medicine to be taken at one time
example_sentences: Always follow the recommended dosage on the medication label.
The dosage needs to be carefully adjusted for younger children.
Never exceed the maximum recommended daily dosage.
words.pronunciation_tip: DOH-sij — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the amount of medicine' every time — say 'the dosage'.

words.definition: the state of being comfortable, healthy, and happy
example_sentences: Regular exercise has a profoundly positive effect on mental well-being.
The company takes employee well-being very seriously indeed.
Adequate sleep is absolutely essential for overall well-being.
words.pronunciation_tip: WEL-bee-ing — stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'health and happiness' every time — say 'well-being'.

words.definition: redness, swelling, and pain in part of the body as a reaction
example_sentences: Applying ice reduces inflammation significantly after a sports injury.
Chronic inflammation can lead to very serious long-term conditions.
Anti-inflammatory drugs help to reduce the pain of swollen joints.
words.pronunciation_tip: in-fluh-MAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the swelling and pain' — say 'inflammation'.

words.definition: involving sitting for long periods with little physical activity
example_sentences: He has a very sedentary office job sitting at a computer all day.
A sedentary lifestyle significantly increases the risk of heart disease.
Too many people in modern life lead sedentary lifestyles.
words.pronunciation_tip: SED-un-ter-ee — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he doesn't move much' formally — say 'he has a sedentary lifestyle'.

words.definition: a continuous dull pain that does not stop
example_sentences: I have a terrible headache that has lasted all afternoon.
My whole body aches terribly after yesterday's long run.
She had a persistent dull ache in her lower back.
words.pronunciation_tip: AYK — rhymes with 'cake'.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have pain in my head' — say 'I have a headache'. Ache = dull, continuous pain.

words.definition: an action taken in advance to prevent something going wrong
example_sentences: She took every possible precaution to avoid getting ill.
As a sensible precaution, they evacuated the whole building.
It's a very sensible precaution to wear factor 50 sunscreen.
words.pronunciation_tip: prih-KAW-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'something to prevent problems' — say 'a precaution' or 'take precautions'.

words.definition: to become progressively worse over time
example_sentences: Her medical condition deteriorated very rapidly and unexpectedly.
The relationship between the two colleagues has seriously deteriorated.
The quality of the equipment had slowly deteriorated.
words.pronunciation_tip: dih-TEER-ee-uh-rayt — five syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'it got worse' in formal contexts — say 'it deteriorated'.

words.definition: to help someone recover their health or abilities after illness
example_sentences: The physiotherapist rehabilitated her knee effectively after surgery.
The hospital has a dedicated unit for rehabilitating patients.
It took him six months to fully rehabilitate after the accident.
words.pronunciation_tip: ree-huh-BIL-ih-tayt — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'help to become healthy again' always — say 'rehabilitate'.

words.definition: a patient receiving hospital treatment without staying overnight
example_sentences: She attends the hospital regularly as an outpatient.
The outpatient clinic operates on Tuesday and Thursday mornings.
Outpatient treatment is usually quicker and more convenient for patients.
words.pronunciation_tip: OWT-pay-shunt — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'patient who doesn't sleep in hospital' — say 'outpatient'.
